---
title: "Tree Species Count Data"
author: "The Class of CSC806"
format: html
---

## Introduction

This report examines species count data and shows the differences between the species. The purpose of the analysis is to compare the differences between the species. The data matters because it showcases the count of the species. Using this data, we can analyze the count and run the appropriate tests given our research question. 

##Background
Trees are really important in ecosystems because they help produce oxygen, store carbon, and provide habitats for many organisms. Some common types of trees include oak, pine, maple, and birch, and each one has its own characteristics and role in the environment.

Oak trees are strong, long-living hardwood trees that produce acorns, which are an important food source for animals. Pine trees are evergreen conifers with needle-like leaves that stay green all year, allowing them to survive in colder and harsher environments. Maple trees are known for their unique leaves and bright fall colors, and some species are used to make maple syrup. Birch trees are easy to recognize because of their white, peeling bark and are often one of the first trees to grow back in disturbed areas.

trees show how different species can adapt to their environments while still playing important roles in maintaining healthy ecosystems.

## Data

This dataset contains counts of different tree species observed in a study area. The variables include species, which identifies the type of tree (oak, pine, maple, birch), and count, which represents the number of trees recorded for each species. The dataset was retrieved from a CSV file on GitHub (retrieved on April 9, 2026) and represents data collected through a field-based ecological survey of tree populations. 

The dataset comes from a simple CSV file included in the repository . It contains counts of different species.

## Analysis

```{r}
# Load data
data <- read.csv("../data/species_counts.csv") # read.csv can read in spreadsheets that are in the .csv

# Calculate total count
total <- sum(data$count) # sum(data$count) adds all the data in the count column

# Print result
total # print the name of the variable, total, which will give the total number of data in the count column

```

Discussion

The results show differences among species, but the interpretation is unclear. The data shows counts of four different tress. The total number of trees is 45, with pine being the most abundant type of tree, followed by oak, maple, and birch. These types of trees are found in Northern Hardwood and Transition Hardwood environments. A higher abundance of pine vs maple and birch suggest a warmer environment, likely Transition Hardwood.
